_note: Beginner-friendly, myth-free definitions. Order matters: longer phrases are matched before shorter ones. Keep every definition consistent with the notebooks — no 'both 0 and 1 at once'.

terms.def: A globe for one qubit. North pole = definitely 0, south pole = definitely 1, and the tilt angle in between sets the bias of the coin.
The gate that turns a 'definitely 0' qubit into a perfect 50/50 fair coin (the |+⟩ state) — it sets the coin spinning.
The rule that turns amplitudes into probabilities: the chance of an outcome is its amplitude squared.
The two numbers (α, β) that describe a qubit. Square them to get the odds of measuring 0 and 1, and they always satisfy |α|²+|β|²=1.
A qubit set to a chosen blend of 0 and 1 — a spinning coin whose bias you pick. It has definite odds, but no definite value until you measure it.
Reading a qubit. It forces the spinning coin to land on a plain 0 or 1. You can't predict one result — only the long-run statistics.
The exact list of a circuit's amplitudes, computed directly — an answer with no measurement noise.
A link between qubits so their results are correlated in ways no independent coins ever could be.
A description of a system's energy. Many problems are solved by finding the state of lowest energy.
A method that nudges circuit angles step by step, letting a classical optimizer 'descend' toward the best answer.
The quantum version of a bit. Undetermined until measured — like a spinning coin before you slap it down. Not 'both 0 and 1 at once'.
Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm — tunes circuit angles to solve hard problems like Max-Cut. The summit of this course.
How many times you run a circuit and measure it. More shots give a clearer picture of the true probabilities.
